text.text:
# Quarterly Business Report - Q4 2025


## Executive Summary

Revenue grew 15% year-over-year to $4.2 billion, driven by strong performance in cloud services and
enterprise software. Operating margin improved to 28%, up from 24% in Q3. Customer acquisition cost
decreased by 12% due to improved marketing efficiency. The company expanded into three new
markets: Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe, and South America.


## Key Metrics

Active users reached 45 million, a 22% increase. Monthly recurring revenue hit $350M. Net promoter
score improved from 62 to 71. Employee headcount grew to 8,500 across 12 offices globally. R&D;
investment was $840M, representing 20% of revenue.


## Outlook

For Q1 2026, we expect revenue between $4.4B and $4.6B. Key initiatives include launching the
AI-powered analytics platform, expanding the partner ecosystem, and achieving SOC 2 Type II
certification for all cloud products.

In this example, the phrase Q4 2025 means the fourth quarter of 2025. Because this phrase includes both the quarter and the year, this phrase assumes that whatever is being analyzed has a fiscal year that is the same as a calendar year.
In this example, the phrase Q4 2025 means the fourth quarter of 2025. Because this phrase includes both the quarter and the year, this phrase assumes that whatever is being analyzed has a fiscal year that is the same as a calendar year. A fiscal calendar is an arbitrary range of dates that defines a company’s annual reporting cycle.
For instance, if a company chooses to have its fiscal year starting in February rather than January, then its first quarter would consist of February, March, and April. Companies sometimes choose to do this if they want their fiscal year to end in their own peak season. Alternatively, since finishing the year often involves a lot of additional accounting work, some companies choose to end their fiscal year on a relatively calm month. There are two components to Q4 2025.
A fiscal quarter is a three-month period on a company’s financial calendar that acts as a basis for reporting earnings and paying dividends.
As its name suggests, there are four quarterly periods in a year, meaning a publicly traded company would issue four quarterly reports per year. Companies and investors alike use fiscal quarters to keep track of their financial results and business developments over time. These quarters are often referred to as Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4.

Earnings season gets underway this week, with reports from major banks providing the first look at corporate performance · The technology sector is expected to be the standout performer with over 25% projected earnings growth, driven by the ongoing "AI arms race" Sectors that rely on lower-end consumer spending are expected to be squeezed again by "value-conscious” shoppers, making the consumer discretionary sector a laggard this season · The Q4 2025 earnings season for US corporations begins this week when big banks kick things off, starting with JPMorgan Chase on Tuesday (Jan 13).

Revenues are expected to have grown 7.7% YoY, the 21st consecutive quarter of growth, and the second-strongest figure in three years. Eight of the 11 S&P sectors are anticipated to post YoY gains, with Information Technology (25.9%), Materials (9.0%) and Financials (6.4%) leading the pack. Only Industrials (-0.5%), Energy (-1.7%) and Consumer Discretionary (-3.5%) are expected to post negative growth.1 · Overall, 2025 EPS growth is expected to come in at 12.4%. For 2026 that figure is anticipated to improve to 14.9%.2

sources.title: Q4 2025 results | News center | ABB
sources.snippet:
I was pleased with the strong free cash flow of $1.5 billion in the quarter. This led to us achieving our ambition to improve annual free cash flow, which reached the record level of $4.6 billion.
In the first quarter of 2026, we anticipate comparable revenue growth in the 7% - 10% range. The operational EBITA margin should increase year-on-year, excluding the announced real estate gains in the first quarters of 2025 and 2026.
Q4 was a strong finish to a record year for ABB. We lead in markets with strong secular trends and we will further build on our ABB Way operating model, which gives me confidence in our updated financial targets and that 2026 will be yet another all-time-high result. Morten Wierod, CEO · We ended the year on a high note and delivered the strongest annual performance yet for ABB. The fourth quarter was a landmark quarter as we for the first time exceeded the $10 billion order level, at $10.3 billion.
Overall, it was reassuring that the strong order development was broad-based with double-digit growth across all three business areas. So, while we delivered the highest quarterly revenues on record, orders were even higher, resulting in a book-to-bill of 1.14.
I was pleased with the strong free cash flow of $1.5 billion in the quarter. This led to us achieving our ambition to improve annual free cash flow, which reached the record level of $4.6 billion. It was also good to see our strong return on capital employed at 25.3%. In the Electrification business area, demand increased in all customer segments, led by data centers which recorded a very strong double-digit growth.

We anticipate 2026 capital expenditures, including principal payments on finance leases, to be in the range of $115-135 billion, with year-over-year growth driven by increased investment to support our Meta Superintelligence Labs efforts and core business. In this example, the phrase Q4 2025 means the fourth quarter of 2025. Because this phrase includes 
both the quarter and the year, this phrase assumes that whatever is being analyzed has a fiscal year
that is the same as a calendar year. A fiscal calendar is an arbitrary range of dates that defines a
company’s annual reporting cycle.
For instance, if a company chooses to have its fiscal year starting in February rather than January,
then its first quarter would consist of February, March, and April. Companies sometimes choose to do
this if they want their fiscal year to end in their own peak season. Alternatively, since finishing 
the year often involves a lot of additional accounting work, some companies choose to end their 
fiscal year on a relatively calm month. There are two components to Q4 2025.
A fiscal quarter is a three-month period on a company’s financial calendar that acts as a basis for 
reporting earnings and paying dividends.
As its name suggests, there are four quarterly periods in a year, meaning a publicly traded company 
would issue four quarterly reports per year. Companies and investors alike use fiscal quarters to 
keep track of their financial results and business developments over time. These quarters are often 
referred to as Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4.

# Quarterly Business Report - Q4 2025


## Executive Summary

Revenue grew 15% year-over-year to $4.2 billion, driven by strong performance in cloud services and
enterprise software. Operating margin improved to 28%, up from 24% in Q3. Customer acquisition cost
decreased by 12% due to improved marketing efficiency. The company expanded into three new
markets: Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe, and South America.


## Key Metrics

Active users reached 45 million, a 22% increase. Monthly recurring revenue hit $350M. Net promoter
score improved from 62 to 71. Employee headcount grew to 8,500 across 12 offices globally. R&D;
investment was $840M, representing 20% of revenue.


## Outlook

For Q1 2026, we expect revenue between $4.4B and $4.6B. Key initiatives include launching the
AI-powered analytics platform, expanding the partner ecosystem, and achieving SOC 2 Type II
certification for all cloud products.

sources.snippet:
In this example, the phrase Q4 2025 means the fourth quarter of 2025. Because this phrase includes both the quarter and the year, this phrase assumes that whatever is being analyzed has a fiscal year that is the same as a calendar year.
In this example, the phrase Q4 2025 means the fourth quarter of 2025. Because this phrase includes both the quarter and the year, this phrase assumes that whatever is being analyzed has a fiscal year that is the same as a calendar year. A fiscal calendar is an arbitrary range of dates that defines a company’s annual reporting cycle.
For instance, if a company chooses to have its fiscal year starting in February rather than January, then its first quarter would consist of February, March, and April. Companies sometimes choose to do this if they want their fiscal year to end in their own peak season. Alternatively, since finishing the year often involves a lot of additional accounting work, some companies choose to end their fiscal year on a relatively calm month. There are two components to Q4 2025.
A fiscal quarter is a three-month period on a company’s financial calendar that acts as a basis for reporting earnings and paying dividends.
As its name suggests, there are four quarterly periods in a year, meaning a publicly traded company would issue four quarterly reports per year. Companies and investors alike use fiscal quarters to keep track of their financial results and business developments over time. These quarters are often referred to as Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4.

In this example, the phrase Q4 2025 means the fourth quarter of 2025. Because this phrase includes 
both the quarter and the year, this phrase assumes that whatever is being analyzed has a fiscal year
that is the same as a calendar year. A fiscal calendar is an arbitrary range of dates that defines a
company’s annual reporting cycle.
For instance, if a company chooses to have its fiscal year starting in February rather than January,
then its first quarter would consist of February, March, and April. Companies sometimes choose to do
this if they want their fiscal year to end in their own peak season. Alternatively, since finishing 
the year often involves a lot of additional accounting work, some companies choose to end their 
fiscal year on a relatively calm month. There are two components to Q4 2025.
A fiscal quarter is a three-month period on a company’s financial calendar that acts as a basis for 
reporting earnings and paying dividends.
As its name suggests, there are four quarterly periods in a year, meaning a publicly traded company 
would issue four quarterly reports per year. Companies and investors alike use fiscal quarters to 
keep track of their financial results and business developments over time. These quarters are often 
referred to as Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4.

# Summary of Q4 2025 Search Findings

## Definition
**Q4 2025** refers to the fourth quarter of 2025, covering **October-December 2025** for companies following a calendar fiscal year. This is a standard three-month reporting period used by businesses to track financial performance.

## Key Corporate Results

### Technology Sector (Strong Performance)
- **SAP**: 30% cloud backlog growth to €77 billion; AI included in 2/3 of Q4 cloud orders
- **Microsoft**: $13.5 billion revenue in More Personal Computing (9% increase); returned $9.4 billion to shareholders
- **Meta**: Strong business performance; planning $115-135 billion capex for 2026
- **NVIDIA**: $39.3 billion revenue (78% YoY growth)
- **Apple**: $102.5 billion quarterly revenue (8% YoY growth)
- **IBM**: Double-digit software growth; $12.5 billion generative AI book of business

### Other Notable Companies
- **Ondas Inc.**: $30.1 million Q4 revenue (605% YoY growth for full year)
- **ABB**: Record $10.3 billion in orders; $4.6 billion annual free cash flow
- **Target**: Revenue and traffic declined for 4th consecutive quarter; net income fell to $1.05 billion

## Market-Wide Trends

### Overall Outlook
- **S&P 500**: Expected 8.3% EPS growth (10th consecutive quarter of growth)
- **Revenue Growth**: 7.7% YoY expected (21st consecutive quarter)
- **Technology sector** leading with 25.9% projected earnings growth

### Banking & Finance
- **FDIC institutions**: $77.7 billion aggregate net income (down 2% from Q3); full-year 2025 net income up 10.2%
- **Direct lending**: $380 billion full-year volume; $248 billion in new-money issuance

### Small Business
- **Small Business Index**: 68.4 (down from Q3's 72.0)
- **70%** report business in good health
- **Inflation** remains top challenge
- **43%** view local economy as healthy

**Q4 2025** refers to the fourth quarter of 2025, covering **October-December 2025** for companies following a calendar fiscal year. This is a standard three-month reporting period used by businesses to track financial performance.

- **Inflation** remains top challenge
- **43%** view local economy as healthy
